content.problemStatement:
Chef is participating in a programming competition. Currently, he has a score of XXX.

There are NNN participants other than Chef.

The iii-th of these participants has a score of AiA_iAi​.

Chef's rank in the contest is determined as follows:


Count the number of indices iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N) such that Ai>XA_i \gt XAi​>X.

Let this count be RRR.
Chef's rank is then equal to R+1R+1R+1.
Chef would like to improve his rank, so he decides to sabotage his opponents.

A sabotage operation is defined as follows:


Choose an index iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), and do **both** of the following:

Set AiA_iAi​ to 000.
Add 100100100 to XXX, that is, increase Chef's score by 100100100.


Since there's not much time remaining, Chef can perform the sabotage operation at most KKK times.

Find the minimum possible rank Chef can achieve at the end.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers N,X,N, X,N,X, and KKK — the number of other participants, Chef's initial score, and the number of times the sabotage operation can be performed, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,…,ANA_1, \ldots, A_NA1​,…,AN​ — the scores of the other participants.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line one integer: the minimum possible rank Chef can achieve.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: K=1K = 1K=1, so at most one person can be sabotaged.
One optimal solution is to choose i=2i = 2i=2. This will set A2=0A_2 = 0A2​=0 and increase XXX by 100100100.
As a result of this, the scores will be A=[250,0,509,12]A = [250, 0, 509, 12]A=[250,0,509,12], and X=150+100=250X = 150+100 = 250X=150+100=250.
Chef's rank here is 222, since there's exactly one person with a strictly score (that being A3=509A_3 = 509A3​=509).
It can be shown that obtaining a rank of <2\lt 2<2 is impossible.
Test case 222: The same set of scores, but K=2K = 2K=2 now.
One optimal solution is to choose i=2i = 2i=2 and i=3i = 3i=3.
As a result of this, the scores will be A=[250,0,0,12]A = [250, 0, 0, 12]A=[250,0,0,12], and X=150+100+100=350X = 150+100+100 = 350X=150+100+100=350.
Chef's rank here is 111, since there's nobody with a higher score than him.
Test case 444: K=0K = 0K=0, so no sabotage can be performed.
All three other participants have a higher score than Chef, so Chef's rank is 3+1=43+1 =43+1=4.
